question.text: In a full ratchet anti-dilution mechanism:

answer.text: The conversion price is adjusted to the weighted average of old and new round prices
The investor's conversion price is reduced to the price of the new lower-priced round entirely
No adjustment is made to the investor's conversion price
The investor receives additional cash compensation

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Full ratchet anti-dilution adjusts the investor's conversion price to exactly the price of the new lower-priced round, regardless of the number of shares issued in the down round. This provides maximum protection to existing investors but is very dilutive to founders. **The Trap:** Full ratchet is more protective than weighted average anti-dilution. In weighted average, the adjustment considers the relative size of the down round, resulting in a less severe price adjustment. **Cross-Exam Context:** Anti-dilution mechanisms (full ratchet vs weighted average) are tested in XIX-A, XIX-C, and XIX-D. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Full ratchet is increasingly rare in Indian PE/VC deals as founders push back. Most deals use broad-based weighted average, which is considered more equitable. Understand which mechanism the fund typically negotiates.
